titleInfo.title: Stories of Miraculous Images and Paying Respect to the Three Jewels: A Discourse on Image Worship in Seventh-Century China

mods.abstract: Shinohara discusses "the discourse on image worship [that] developed gradually and in a variety of separate and disconnected directions over a long period in Buddhism" on the basis of works by Daoxuan (596-667) and Daoshi (dates unknown): The Jade Forest in the Dharma Garden (Fayuan zhulin, T. 2122, 53) and the Essential Teachings of Scriptures [Zhujing yaoyi, T. 2123, 54] by Daoshi, as well as “Paying respect to monks and images” (sengxiang zhijing) in Daoxuan’s vinaya commentary, Sifenlü shanfan bujue xingshi chao (T. 1804). He concludes that "image worship, or paying respect to images, is highlighted as a crucial element in a defense of the autonomy of the Samgha" that was threatened again by two Tang emperors. "Taizong’s edict requiring monks and nuns to ”pay respect” (or “pay homage,” zhijing) to parents and the ruler was issued in 631, although it was quickly withdrawn in 633... In 662, Gaozong issued an order to have the matter of requiring monks and nuns do reverence (zhibai) to the emperor, the empress, the crown prince, and parents be publicly debated (T. 2108: 52.455ab; T. 2103: 52.284a)." He tries to reconcile the fact that in Daoshi’s longer anthology Jade Forest in the Dharma Garden lots of miracle stories about wonder-working icons are included, while in the shorter anthology Essential Teachings of Scriptures mainly quotations from the Guanfo sanmei[hai] jing (The Ocean-like Samādhi of Buddha Meditation, T. 643: 15.688b, 688c-689a) dealing with visualizations and mental images are presented. He seems to be irritated about the fact that in the Essential Teachings of Scriptures "image worship is conceived of simply as a preliminary step in the practice of Buddha visualization, and the linkage between image worship and the defense of the Samgha is no longer visible." I propose that for his Essential Teachings of Scriptures, Daoshi thought that only the mental practice of Buddha visualization would be appropriate. As Daoxuan had pointed out in his vinaya commentary earlier, paying respect to images was not considered the highest form of “seeing the Buddha". That was seeing the Dharma body by means of contemplation on emptiness, like Subhuti did when he surpassed the nun Utpalavarnā in paying respect to the Buddha (須菩提觀諸法空是為見佛法身。得真供養供養中最。非以致敬生身為供養也) according to the Dazhidulun.

titleInfo.title: Images in Asian Religions: Texts and Contexts
